hook.script: Imagine a single musical note that could be both loud and soft at the same time. That's superposition – a key to quantum computing's power.
visualCues: Close-up of a violin string vibrating
Sound waves visualised as overlapping patterns

context.script: Classical computers use bits, like on/off switches. Quantum computers use qubits. But qubits can be in multiple states simultaneously, thanks to superposition and entanglement.
visualCues: Binary code (0s and 1s) appearing on screen
Qubit represented as a sphere with a pointer
Two qubits linked by a visual connection

segments.title: Superposition
segments.script: A qubit exists in a combination of 0 and 1. It's like a chord on a piano, a blend of different notes existing together until measured.
visualCues: Visualisation of a chord's frequency spectrum

segments.title: Entanglement
segments.script: Entangled qubits are linked. Change one, and the other instantly changes too, regardless of distance, like two perfectly tuned instruments resonating together.
visualCues: Two tuning forks vibrating in unison

recap.script: Qubits use superposition and entanglement to perform calculations impossible for regular computers. This enables breakthroughs in medicine and materials science.

blocks.title: Qubits, Superposition, and Entanglement
blocks.heroPrompt: Quantum computer circuit board with glowing connections between qubits, viewed at a slight angle.
blocks.description: Explore the foundational principles of quantum computing: qubits, superposition, and entanglement. Understand how these concepts enable quantum computers to solve complex problems.
objectives: Describe what a qubit is and how it differs from a bit
Outline the potential applications of quantum computing

blocks.quote: Quantum computers can solve problems that would take classical computers longer than the age of the universe.

blocks.label: Learning Objective
blocks.text: After this lesson, you will be able to describe **qubits**, explain **superposition** and **entanglement**, and understand their role in **quantum computation**.

blocks.title: Visual Insight: Quantum Superposition
blocks.videoPrompt: A mixing console with many faders moving smoothly and automatically.
blocks.caption: Qubits can exist in multiple states at once, allowing quantum computers to perform many calculations simultaneously.

cards.imagePrompt: Binary code stream fading into a circuit board.
cards.title: Classical Bit
cards.description: A classical bit is the fundamental unit of information in classical computers. It can be either 0 or 1.

cards.imagePrompt: Qubit represented as a sphere with a needle pointing to a position on its surface.
cards.title: Qubit
cards.description: A qubit is the fundamental unit of information in quantum computers. It can be 0, 1, or a superposition of both.

cards.imagePrompt: Entangled pair of qubits with a visual link between them.
cards.title: Entanglement
cards.description: Entanglement is a quantum phenomenon where two qubits become linked, and the state of one instantly influences the state of the other, regardless of distance.

insights.title: Superposition Isn't Just Randomness
insights.body: It's a specific *combination* of states. Like a musical chord, it's not just noise, but a structured blend of frequencies.

insights.title: Entanglement Isn't Faster-Than-Light Communication
insights.body: Measuring one entangled qubit *does* instantly affect the other, but you can't use it to send a message faster than light.

insights.title: Quantum Computers Aren't Replacing Classical Computers
insights.body: They excel at specific types of problems. Your laptop won't be quantum any time soon.

blocks.heading: The Quantum Leap: Qubits
paragraphs: Imagine a light switch. It can be either on or off, representing 1 or 0 – the basis of classical computing. Now, picture a dimmer switch. It can be on, off, or *anywhere in between*. That's a qubit. Unlike a classical bit, which is either 0 or 1, a qubit can be in a *superposition* of both states simultaneously.

This 'in-between' state is where the magic happens. It allows quantum computers to perform many calculations at once. Think of it like an orchestra playing all possible melodies at the same time. The 'best' melody is then amplified through clever quantum algorithms. Qubits are physical systems, such as ions, photons, or superconducting circuits, that obey the laws of quantum mechanics.

The power of quantum computing comes from manipulating and measuring these qubits. Quantum algorithms exploit superposition and another quantum phenomenon called *entanglement* to solve problems that are intractable for classical computers. A key concept is *quantum interference*: like sound waves adding or cancelling each other, quantum states can be manipulated to enhance the probability of the desired outcome.

cards.imagePrompt: Sheet music with clearly defined notes and rests.
cards.title: Classical Computing
cards.description: Uses bits, which are like individual, distinct musical notes. Each calculation is performed one after another.

cards.imagePrompt: Orchestra tuning up, with many instruments playing simultaneously.
cards.title: Quantum Computing
cards.description: Uses qubits, which are like chords – combinations of notes played at once. Many calculations are performed simultaneously.

cards.title: Ion Qubits
cards.description: Use trapped ions, atoms with an electrical charge, to represent qubits.

cards.title: Superconducting Qubits
cards.description: Employ tiny superconducting circuits cooled to near absolute zero.

cards.title: Photonic Qubits
cards.description: Utilise photons, individual particles of light, to encode quantum information.

steps.label: Prepare
steps.description: Create two qubits in a special entangled state.

steps.label: Separate
steps.description: Move the entangled qubits far apart.

steps.label: Measure
steps.description: Measure the state of one qubit.

steps.label: Determine
steps.description: The state of the other qubit is instantly known.

blocks.description: A qubit exists as a combination of states, not simply 0 or 1.

blocks.question: What happens when you measure a qubit in superposition?
options: It becomes entangled with another qubit.
blocks.reveal: When measured, a qubit in superposition 'collapses' into a definite state of either 0 or 1. The probability of each outcome is determined by the initial superposition.

blocks.scenario: A pharmaceutical company wants to discover a new drug.
blocks.challenge: Simulating molecular interactions to identify promising drug candidates is computationally expensive for classical computers.
blocks.resolution: Quantum computers can simulate these interactions much more efficiently, accelerating drug discovery.

blocks.heading: Real-World Applications
blocks.intro: Quantum computing is poised to revolutionise various industries. Here are a few examples.

tabs.label: Medicine
tabs.imageCaption: Analysing medical images with quantum precision.
tabs.scenarioBody: Quantum computers can simulate molecular interactions, accelerating the discovery of new <mark>life-saving drugs</mark>.

tabs.label: Materials Science
tabs.imagePrompt: Scientist examining a new material in a lab.
tabs.imageCaption: Designing novel materials with specific properties.
tabs.scenarioBody: Quantum simulations can help design new materials with desired properties, such as <mark>high-temperature superconductors</mark>.

tabs.label: Finance
tabs.imageCaption: Optimising investment portfolios with quantum algorithms.
tabs.scenarioBody: Quantum algorithms can optimise investment portfolios and improve risk management in the <mark>financial sector</mark>.

tabs.label: Cryptography
tabs.scenarioBody: Quantum computers threaten current encryption methods. They also offer new methods for <mark>quantum-secure communication</mark>.

blocks.title: Quantum Supremacy
blocks.text: Quantum supremacy (or advantage) doesn't mean quantum computers can solve *all* problems faster than classical computers. It means they can solve *specific* problems faster.

blocks.instruction: Improve the following explanation of superposition:
blocks.weakPrompt: Superposition means a qubit is sometimes 0 and sometimes 1.
blocks.modelAnswer: Superposition means a qubit exists in a *combination* of possible states (0 and 1) *simultaneously*, like a piano string vibrating with multiple harmonics. Measuring the qubit forces it to 'collapse' into a single, definite state.

blocks.title: If you remember only three things...
points: Qubits use superposition to represent multiple states at once.
Entanglement links qubits, allowing for correlated computations.
Quantum computers can solve problems intractable for classical computers.

imageContext.imagePrompt: Qubit represented as a sphere with a needle pointing to a position on its surface.

options.text: A qubit being in a combination of 0 and 1 simultaneously.

questions.correctFeedback: Correct! Superposition allows a qubit to exist in multiple states at once.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Incorrect. Superposition is the ability of a qubit to be in multiple states simultaneously.

imageContext.imagePrompt: Entangled pair of qubits with a visual link between them.

questions.questionText: True or false: Measuring one entangled qubit instantly affects the state of the other, regardless of distance.
questions.correctFeedback: Correct! This is the essence of quantum entanglement.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Incorrect. This is a key characteristic of quantum entanglement.

imageContext.imagePrompt: Quantum computer circuit board with glowing connections between qubits.

questions.questionText: Quantum computers excel at solving problems that are __________ for classical computers.
questions.correctFeedback: Correct! Quantum computers are designed to tackle problems that are computationally infeasible for classical computers.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Quantum computers are designed to solve problems that classical computers struggle with.

terms.definition: The basic unit of information in quantum computing, existing in a superposition of states.
The ability of a qubit to be in multiple states (0 and 1) simultaneously.
A quantum phenomenon where two qubits become linked, and their fates are intertwined.
A computational procedure designed to run on a quantum computer.
The point where a quantum computer can solve a specific problem faster than any classical computer.

keyTakeaway: Qubits, superposition, and entanglement are the fundamental building blocks of quantum computing.
discussionPrompt: What are the potential ethical implications of quantum computing?
nextTopicTeaser: Learn how quantum gates manipulate qubits to perform computations.
